coredata.dc:title: SERS detection of Hg2+ and aflatoxin B1 through on–off strategy of oxidase-like Au@HgNPs/carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Xiaorong
Li, Qiulan
Li, Hong
Wang, Yijie
Xiao, Feijian
Yang, Dezhi
Xia, Qinghai
Yang, Yaling
coredata.dc:description: In this work, cerium-doped carbon dots (Ce-CDs) both as a reducing agent and template hybrid gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) with weak oxidase-like (OXD) activity was synthesized for the detection of Hg2+ and aflatoxin B1 (AFB1). The AuNPs can catalyze efficiently mercury ion (Hg2+) reduction to the metallic (Hg0) to form Au-Hg amalgam (Au@HgNPs). The obtained Au@HgNPs with strong OXD-like activity oxidize without Raman-active leucomalachite green (LMG) into the Raman-active malachite green (MG) and simultaneously as the SERS substrates by the formed Raman “hot spot” through MG-induced Au@HgNPs aggregation. While AFB1 was introduced resulting in the SERS intensity decreasing due to Hg2+ with AFB1 via carbonyl group to inhibit the aggregation of Au@HgNPs. The work paves a new path for the design of a nanozyme-based SERS protocol for tracing Hg2+ and AFB1 residues in foodstuff analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hg2+
AFB1
Au-Hg amalgam
Oxidase-like activity
Leucomalachite green
SERS

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The preparation of cerium-doped carbon dots (Ce-CDs)
SERS measurements of Hg2+ and AFB1
The principle of SERS sensing Hg2+ and AFB1
Sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+ and AFB1
Possible detection mechanism of Hg2+ and AFB1

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was greatly supported by Yunnan Fundamental Research Projects (No. 202201AU070117), Yunnan Fundamental Research Projects (No. 202001AT070002), Major Science and Technology Project of Yunnan Province (No. 202002AE320006), Joint Specialized Fund for Applied Fundamental Research Program of the Department of Science and Technology of Yunnan Province – Kunming Medical University (No. 2018FE001(-133)).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of Ce-CDs/AuNPs (a) and Au@HgNPs (b); (c) HAADF-STEM, elemental mapping images of C, Ce, Au and Hg; (d) FTIR spectra of nanozymes; (e) XRD spectra of nanozymes; (f-i) XPS spectra of nanozymes.
(a) Absorption spectra of different substrates being oxidized; (b) Absorption spectra of LMG being oxidized; (c) The SERS signal of LMG being oxidized.
(a) SERS spectra with the different concentration of Hg2+; (b) linear curve of Hg2+ concentration; (c) SERS spectra with the different concentration of AFB1; (d) linear curve of AFB1 concentration.
(a) Sensitive of Au@HgNPs-LMG system for Hg2+ detection; (b) Sensitive of Au@HgNPs-LMG system for AFB1 detection; (c) Selective detection of Ce-CDs/AuNPs for Hg2+ detection.

(a) ζ-potential for surface charge measurement; (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of AuNPs and Au@HgNPs; (c) Comparison of SERS signals in the order of Hg2+ and LMG addition; (d) Verification of UV–vis spectra of ∙O2 – production in the reaction system with INT; (e) EPR spectra for the detection of ∙O2 – in Ce-CDs/AuNPs and Au@HgNPs system.
Schematic illustration of the sensing of Hg2+ and AFB1 involving the Au@HgNPs.

head.ce:title: SERS detection of Hg2+ and aflatoxin B1 through on–off strategy of oxidase-like Au@HgNPs/carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Yunnan Province 650500, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Life Science and Technology
Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Yunnan Province 650500, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Public Health, Kunming Medical University, Kunming 650500, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Public Health, Kunming Medical University, Kunming 650500, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The author contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, cerium-doped carbon dots (Ce-CDs) both as a reducing agent and template hybrid gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) with weak oxidase-like (OXD) activity was synthesized for the detection of Hg2+ and aflatoxin B1 (AFB1). The AuNPs can catalyze efficiently mercury ion (Hg2+) reduction to the metallic (Hg0) to form Au-Hg amalgam (Au@HgNPs). The obtained Au@HgNPs with strong OXD-like activity oxidize without Raman-active leucomalachite green (LMG) into the Raman-active malachite green (MG) and simultaneously as the SERS substrates by the formed Raman “hot spot” through MG-induced Au@HgNPs aggregation. While AFB1 was introduced resulting in the SERS intensity decreasing due to Hg2+ with AFB1 via carbonyl group to inhibit the aggregation of Au@HgNPs. The work paves a new path for the design of a nanozyme-based SERS protocol for tracing Hg2+ and AFB1 residues in foodstuff analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that mercury ion (Hg2+) is one of the most toxic and harmful heavy metal ions (Zhai et al., 2022; Singh et al., 2019). Most SERS are used for the detection of Hg2+ in water, but fewer methods are used for detection of Hg2+ in food or some agricultural crops (Song et al., 2022; Guo et al., 2019). Therefore, developing highly sensitive and selective sensors for reliable and rapid detection of trace mercury ions is an urgent need.

Aflatoxin is mainly produced by Aspergillus parasiticus secondary metabolites, the highest probability of aflatoxin in food and feed in hot and humid regions (Chen et al., 2018a). Among the most predominant aflatoxins in food (aflatoxins B1, B2, G1 and G2), aflatoxins B1(AFB1) is classified as a Group I carcinogen by the International Agency for Research on Cancer (Jiao et al., 2021; Mukherjee et al., 2018). Therefore, the detection of trace amounts of AFB1 has become an important issue in public health care. Detection methods for Hg2+ and AFB1 include atomic fluorescence spectrometry (AFS) (Sun et al., 2018), inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) (Liu et al., 2013), high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Hu et al., 2018), liquid chromatography/mass spectrometry (LC-MS) (Xue et al., 2014), and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (Zhang et al., 2018). There are few methods for detecting AFB1 by SERS, and even fewer studies are found in the area of AFB1 modulation of SERS signal intensity. In addition, these methods are usually cumbersome, time-consuming in sample pre-treatment, and expensive. Therefore, there is a need to develop more convenient, accurate and sensitive analytical ways for the rapid detection of Hg2+ and AFB1 (Maity et al., 2014; Chen et al., 2007; Liu et al., 2021).

Surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) has testified to be a strong analytical tool with fast, sensitive and selective characteristics, which can achieve trace analysis even at a single-molecule level (Yao et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2018). SERS sensors usually focuses on plasmonic metal nanostructures (Au, Ag, and Cu) with sizes over 30 nm exhibiting excellent SERS substrates with high enhancement factors (Makam et al., 2018; Wei and Huang, 2018; Deng et al., 2018; Kamińska et al., 2018). Recently, some nanoparticles have been prepared to act as SERS substrates with a satisfactory mimic enzyme activity for monitoring the catalytic process of catalytic reactions (Yuan et al., 2022; Huang et al., 2022; Xia et al., 2021; Li et al., 2020).

Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) are one of the materials often used in nanotechnology with SERS properties and inherent enzyme-like multifunctionality (Liang et al., 2017; Wan et al., 2020). However, it is limited by the low enzyme-like activity and the poor colloidal stability of the AuNPs resulting in a weak signal. The application is hampered, so more efforts are needed to improve the enzyme-like activity.

Herein, AuNPs with weak oxidase (OXD)-mimicking activity were prepared by using Ce-CDs both as a reducing agent and a template. Notably, the introducing Hg2+ can result in a distinctly enhanced OXD-like performance of the AuNPs, attributing to Hg2+ reduced to the metallic (Hg0) forming Au@HgNPs. Thereby, Au@HgNPs oxidize leucomalachite green (LMG) to malachite green (MG) with a strong Raman signal for detecting Hg2+ by SERS. Since AFB1 can inhibit the massive aggregation of Au@HgNPs particles by oxygen atoms on the carbonyl group, a reduction of SERS intensity was observed in the selective detection of AFB1. The bioenzyme-free SERS assay based on Au@HgNPs nanozyme is used to detect Hg2+ and AFB1 through an on–off strategy (Scheme 1 ). The proposed strategy salves the weak catalytic activity and poor stability of AuNPs.

Gold(III) chloride trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O, ≥99.9%), Cerium nitrate hexahydrate (Ce(NO3)3⋅6H2O, 99.5%), Dopamine hydrochloride(DA, 98%), citric acid (CA), 1,2-ethyl-enediamine (EDA, >99%), 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine(TMB), o-phenylenediamine(OPD), 2,2′-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS), leucomalachite green (LMG), and malachite green (MG) were purchased from Aladdin Reagents Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Mercury ion (Hg2+) was obtained from the National Center for Reference Materials (Beijing, China). Aflatoxin B1 (AFB1), aflatoxin B2 (AFB2), aflatoxin G1 (AFG1), aflatoxin G2 (AFG2), fumonisin B1 (FB1), fumonisin B2 (FB2), zearalenone (ZEN), patulin and were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai) Trading Co., Ltd in China. All solutions were prepared with deionized (DI, 18.2 MΩ) water which was obtained through a Milli-Q water purification system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The preparation of cerium-doped carbon dots (Ce-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Ce-CDs were synthesized by a one-step microwave digestion method (Multiwave PM 60, Jena, Germany). Briefly, 0.1633 g of Ce(NO3)3·6H2O, 0.0309 g of DA and 2.1 g of CA were dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water, followed by the addition of 50 µL of EDA to the above solution and stirred and mixed. The reaction mixture was then placed in a Teflon reactor and the temperature was raised to 180°C within 1 min for 60 min under 850 W microwave irradiation. After the reactor was cooled to room temperature, the product was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane. The filtrate was stored at 4°C and used for further experiments.

Briefly, 30 μL of the prepared Ce-CDs (0.2 mg mL−1) was added to the mixture solution include 200 μL HAuCl4 (1%) and 30 mL of DI water, and stirred at temperature of 90℃ for 5 min. After that, 0.1 mM of trisodium citrate was added and stirred at 90°C for 20 min. Stirring was completed and brought to room temperature. Finally, the Ce-CDs/AuNPs powder was obtained by drying in a vacuum drying oven at a temperature of 60°C.

The prepared Ce-CDs/AuNPs and Au@HgNPs were vacuum dried to obtain fine solid powders, which were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Bruker Tensor27), X-ray diffraction (XRD, UltimalIV) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha Nexsa) were performed for characterization. Enzyme activity was measured by ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV-2550). The morphological changes of Ce-CDs/AuNPs and Au@HgNPs were studied by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, TF20). The Raman signals were detected by a portable Raman spectrometer (SERS, SSR-3000).

ce:section.ce:section-title: SERS measurements of Hg2+ and AFB1
ce:section.ce:para: For Hg2+ measurement, 50 µg L−1 of different volumes (0.625–90 µg L−1) of Hg2+ solution were added to 100 μL of water spiked with AuNPs. And then the mixture was shaken well for 10 min of reaction. A solution containing 20 µL of LMG (5 mM) was added and incubated for 2 min, and Raman spectra were collected using an EZ Raman-SSR 3000 spectrometer. For AFB1 measurement, 50 µL of different concentrations (0.125–87.5 µg L−1) of AFB1 solution were added to 100 µL of AuNPs in water, and the mixture was shaken well for 2 min of reaction, and 20 µL of Hg2+ (50 µg L−1) solution was added. The mixture was then shaken well and reacted for 10 min. Finally, a solution containing 20 µL of LMG (5 mM) was added and incubated for 2 min and Raman spectra were collected using an EZ Raman-SSR 3000 spectrometer. Before collecting the Raman spectra, the parameters were set as follows: 785 nm laser source, the spectral resolution of 12 cm−1, accumulation time of 10 s, and spectral average of 3 times. The Raman shift range in this mode was from 200 to 1800 cm−1.

OXD-like activity was assessed by performing coloring reactions using typical TMB, OPD ABTS and LMG substrates. The total volume was 3 mL, which contains 100 μL of Ce-CDs/AuNPs and 100 μL Hg2+ (50 µg L−1), 100 μL of the substrate (5 mM), and 400 μL of buffer (NaAc-HAc, pH = 4).

In a typical OXD-like reaction, the enzyme activity was evaluated overall by colorimetric reaction using TMB as substrate. A reaction mixture containing 500 μL of pH = 4 buffer, 100 μL of 1 mg mL−1 Au@HgNPs was exposed to different volume of 5 mM TMB (20–200 μL). Finally, the total reaction volume was kept at 3 mL. The changes in absorbance values of the catalytic system were monitored at 654 nm in a UV–vis spectrometer. The steady-state kinetic parameters (Km and Vmax ) were calculated by means of a Lineweaver-Burk plot with the double inverse of the Michaelis-Menten equation:
1 v
=
K m
V
m a x
×
1
S
+
1
V
m a x
where v is the initial velocity, [S] is the concentration of TMB, Km is the Michaelis-Menten constant, and Vmax is the maximum velo city.

Pretreatment of Hg2+ samples: Purchased four different samples from local supermarkets. Weigh 0.5 g of ground pepper, peanut and corn powder in a microwave digestion tank, add 5 mL of mixed solution of nitric acid and hydrogen peroxide (v/v = 1:3), and heat at 180℃ for 30 min after the reaction. Then the digestion jar was put to room temperature to cool down, followed by heating the digestion liquid for 1 h to completely evaporate the acid in its solution. Finally, the pH of the digestion solution was then adjusted to 7.0 with hydrochloric acid and sodium hydroxide solution.

Pretreatment of AFB1 samples: Weigh 1 g of the sample in a 10 mL test tube, then the samples were extracted with 4 mL extracting solution (methanol–water; 7:3, v/v) by vortex mixing for 3 min and sonicated for 20 min, and then centrifuged at 8000 r/min for 10 min. 4 mL of the supernatant was filtered sequentially through a filter paper with a pore size of 0.22 μm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The principle of SERS sensing Hg2+ and AFB1
ce:section.ce:para: Ce-CDs/AuNPs can catalyze the oxidation of LMG to SERS-active MG. The addition of Hg2+ results in a significant enhancement of OXD-like activity and Raman spectra due to the formation of gold amalgam (Au@HgNPs). Ce-CDs/AuNPs are prepared by using Ce-CDs as reducing, and added Hg2+ is catalyzed and reduced to Hg0, forming gold amalgam aggregates covering the surface of Ce-CDs/AuNPs. When AFB1 was introduced into the system, the quenching of SERS intensity was observed due to the inhibition of the aggregation of Au@HgNPs sensors by AFB1 through carbonyl oxygen atoms. The particles were dispersed when Hg2+ was incubated with AFB1 before binding to AuNPs. More importantly, the monodisperse Ce-CDs/AuNPs have lower OXD-like activity and SERS intensity. By characterization (Jiao et al., 2021), it was strongly demonstrated that ion-triggered aggregation controlled the SERS intensity, which increased the complexation between AFB1 and Hg2+, revealing that AFB1 could inhibit the aggregation of Au@HgNPs thereby reducing the SERS signal intensity. One reason is that Au@HgNPs increased in size, while the rough surface of Au@HgNPs led to an increase in SERS signal intensity. Another reason is that a new SERS signal was observed at 794 cm−1 due to the newly formed C-Hg stretching vibration. Therefore, a new SERS method for sensing Hg2+ and AFB1 was developed. The response can be depicted in Scheme 1. For the Hg2+ sensor, the SERS intensity increases with increasing Hg2+ concentration; for aflatoxin B1, the SERS intensity decreases with increasing AFB1 concentration.

As shown in Fig. 1 a-i, the morphologies of Ce-CDs/AuNPs and Au@HgNPs was characterized via TEM. TEM images shows that Ce-CDs/AuNPs were highly monodispersed and had an average diameter of 0.2 ± 0.03 nm, and the corresponding Ce-CDs/AuNPs image was displayed in Fig. 1a. The HRTEM image of Ce-CDs/AuNPs and Au@HgNPs show that the lattice fringe spacing was 0.22 nm and 0.25 nm, respectively, which was in good agreement with the crystal plane spacing of Au(111) (Fig. 1a and b). While the formation of Au@HgNPs amalgam was evident with the presence of large particle aggregates fused together after Hg2+ incubation.

The formation of Au-Hg nanostructures was observed from HRTEM images (Fig. S1a and b), and elemental mapping using HAADF-STEM imaging (Fig. 1c) indicates the Au and Hg presence of nanostructure. The EDS elemental composition and content can be further confirmed by the EDS spectroscopy (Fig. S1c), highlighting the presence of both Au and Hg in the same fused Au-Hg nanostructure.

The FTIR spectra (Fig. 1d) of Ce-CDs/AuNPs and Au@HgNPs show IR peaks are generated at 3521, 1604, 1236, 1058 and 831 cm−1, are represented the stretching vibrations of bonds with OH, CH, C-OH and CC, respectively. In the XRD patterns of Ce-CDs, Ce-CDs/AuNPs and Au@HgNPs (Fig. 1e), typical 2θ peaks appear at 38.9°, 46.5°, 66.2° and 77.5°, which are assigned to the (111), (200), (220) and (311), and are consistent with the HRTEM and EDX results, respectively(Liang et al., 2022).

To further validatethe morphologies and formation of Ce-CDs/AuNPs and Au@HgNPs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis were carried out to analyze the surface composition of the Ce-CDs/AuNPs before and after reaction to Hg2+. The XPS of Ce-CDs/AuNPs and Au@HgNPs exhibit in Fig. 1f-i, the four typical characteristic peaks are response for typical C, N, Ce and Au elements for Ce-CDs/AuNPs, and the five typical characteristic peaks are response for typical C, N, Ce, Au and Hg elements for Au@HgNPs (Chen et al., 2018b), respectively. The binding energies C 1 s, N 1 s,Ce 3d and Au 4f of bands with the induction of Hg2+ move to the binding energy location, indicating that the Ce adsorption enhances after Hg2+ involves in the reaction (Jimenez-Falcao et al., 2020).

High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s and N1s were also fitted (Fig. S1d and e). The Ce 3d spectrum fits three peaks at 881.9, 902.7 and 914.2 eV for Ce-CDs/AuNPs and 880.1, 889.8 and 902.0 eV for Au@HgNPs (Fig. 1g). The high-resolution Au 4f XPS spectrum was divided into two of peaks (Fig. 1h): the binding energies at 83.4 and 87.1 eV for Ce-CDs/AuNPs and Au@HgNPs at 83.6 and 87.5 eV are the Au 4f7/2 and Au 4f5/2 of metal Au0 and the complete reduction of Au precursors, respectively (Greczynski and Hultman, 2019; Tauson et al., 2010). As shown in Fig. 1i, the XPS measurements indicate that high binding energies of Hg 4f bands are seen after the induction of Hg2+ (104.3 and 100.2 eV), which attribute to Hg 4f7/2 and 4f5/2 of C-Hg bonds, respectively (Liu et al., 2021).

The enzyme-activity of OXD was evaluated using TMB, ABTS and OPD as chromogenic substrates. As shown in Fig. 2 a, these colorless chromogenic substrates could be oxidized under the catalysis of Ce-CDs/AuNPs + Hg2+ system forming Au-Hg amalgam, and the corresponding blue, green and yellow colors, and the corresponding characteristic peaks were 654, 416 and 421 nm, respectively.

In this work, OXD-like activity was evaluated using LMG as a substrate. As shown in Fig. 2b, LMG can be oxidized under the catalysis of Ce-CDs/AuNPs and Au@HgNPs and forms a corresponding blue color with a clear absorption peak at 619 nm. It can be found that the absorbance is significantly higher under Au@HgNPs catalysis than that under Ce-CDs/AuNPs catalysis. As shown in Fig. 2c, the Raman signal of LMG catalyzed by Au@HgNPs was significantly higher than that catalyzed by Ce-CDs/AuNPs. These results also indicated that the sensor system has a low background (noise signal) and its good performance.

Here, UV–vis-kinetics and SERS-kinetics models were used to monitor the catalytic efficiency of the POD-like reaction according to reference (Wen et al., 2020).The catalytic mechanism of the catalytic activity of the Ce-CDs/AuNPs and Au@HgNPs, a steady-state kinetic reaction was performed by changing the concentrations of TMB while keeping other concentrations constant. To well known, enzyme-mimicking response usually generate on catalyst surfaces, while UV–vis-kinetics only show the oxidation of substrate molecules in the solution. And SERS-kinetics provide in situ survey of the actual enzyme-mimicking catalytic reaction on a catalyst surface. The Km of Ce-CDs/AuNPs nanozyme was calculated as 0.29 mM for TMB by UV–vis absorption measurement, 0.23 mM for TMB by SERS measurement. The Km of Au@HgNPs nanozyme was calculated as 0.13 mM for TMB by UV–vis absorption measurement, 0.16 mM for TMB by SERS measurement (Fig. S2). The results of UV–vis absorption measurement areconsistent with SERS measurements. The results display between Au@HgNPs and TMB was stronger comparing Ce-CDs/AuNPs and TMB (Table S1). These results offered dependable witness for the high catalytic efficiency of Au@HgNPs, due to the occurrence of more “active sites” and “hot spots” on the surface of the Au@HgNPs.

As shown in Fig. S3a, the SERS signal of LMG on Au@HgNPs nanozymes shows oxidized LMG with a strong SERS signal at 794 cm−1 in the presence of Hg2+, which comes from the newly formed C-Hg stretching vibration. In the presence of AFB1, the SERS signal is reduced at 875 cm−1 as shown in Fig. S3b. This spectrum is due to the inhibition of gold amalgam aggregation by AFB1.

The Au@HgNPs nanozyme was also used as an efficient SERS substrate for the study with LMG as reporter probe. Factors affecting SERS detection include pH, LMG concentration, concentration of Ce-CDs/AuNPs, and reaction time. Fig. S4a–d show that the optimal pH for Hg2+ was 4, the concentration of Ce-CDs/AuNPs was 0.28 mg/mL−1, the concentration of LMG was 1 mM and the reaction time was 2 min; the optimal pH for aflatoxin B1 was 5, the concentration of Ce-CDs/AuNPs was 0.5 mg/mL−1, the concentration of LMG was 0.52 mM and the reaction time was 7 min. The switching strategy was based on the Hg2+-stimulated OXD-like property, where LMG was oxidized to MG and the SERS intensity increased with increasing Hg2+ concentration at 794 cm−1 (Fig. 3 a and b); the introduced AFB1 inhibited the aggregation of Au@HgNPs sensor by carbonyl oxygen atoms, and the intensity of SERS at 875 cm−1 decreased with increasing AFB1 concentration (Fig. 3c and d). A linear plot was made from the obtained data SERS intensity versus Hg2+ concentration (Fig. 3b) in the range of 0.625–90 µg L−1 with the regression equation Int = 22.73[Hg2+] (µg L−1) + 1429.91 (R2 = 0.996). Based on the signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) 3, the limit of detection was 0.147 µg L−1, which is well below the maximum allowable level of 10 μg L−1 of Hg2+ in the food according to the GB 2762-94. As shown in Table S2, the method proposed in this study showed higher sensitivity and a comparably low detection limit (LOD) (0.147 µg L-1) to those of the reported other assays. The developed Au@HgNPs-based SERS assay showed comparable analytical performances in the detection of Hg2+.

The collected SERS intensities versus AFB1 concentrations (Fig. 3d) were plotted linearly in the range of 0.125–87.5 µg L−1, with a regression equation of Int = -55.79[AFB1] (µg L−1) + 8912.72 (R2 = 0.992). Based on the signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) 3, the limit of detection was 0.08 µg L−1, which is well below the maximum allowable level of 20 μg L−1 of AFB1 in peanut oil as stipulated by the GB 2761-2011. As shown in Table S3, comparing the linear range and detection limits of the strategy of this study with those of some detection methods for AFB1 reported in the literature, it can be further confirmed that this sensor has a wider linear range and better stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+ and AFB1
ce:section.ce:para: Some metal ions and anions were added to the system (same concentration as Hg2+) to verify the selectivity of the proposed method (50 times the LOD). As seen in Fig. 4a, the addition of Hg2+ to the reaction solution resulted in a significant SERS signal at 794 cm−1 compared to the other ions. Therefore, the SERS sensing of Au@HgNPs can specifically identify Hg2+ in real food samples. Seven toxins such as AFB2, AFG1, AFG2, fumonisin B1 (FB1), fumonisin B2 (FB2), zearalenone (ZEN) and bacitracin were used as interfering substances. As shown in Fig. 4b, the changes in SERS signal intensity were in low level in the presence of AFB1, AFB2, AFG1, and AFG2 due to these four aflatoxins with homogeneous structure of carbonyl oxygen atom as the binding site and thus similar influences the SERS intensity. And the SERS response were unchanged for the other fungal toxins on the mercury-induced aggregation. This result indicates that the detection system has an outstanding selectivity for the quantification of aflatoxins and AFB1 (Jiao et al., 2021).

As shown in Fig. 4 c, the addition of Hg2+ to the reaction solution produced a significant SERS signal at 794 cm−1 compared to the blank, and the addition of methylmercury produced no SERS signal. Therefore, SERS sensing of Ce-CDs/AuNPs can specifically identify Hg2+ in some food samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible detection mechanism of Hg2+ and AFB1
ce:section.ce:para: The observed high SERS signal might be due to the electrostatic interaction between the positively charged Hg2+ and the negatively charged Ce-CDs/AuNPs leading to the generation of hot spots. The increase in the SERS signal of the Au@HgNPs + LMG system and the fact that AFB1 can decrease the SERS signal of the system can be explained by the ζ-potential (Fig. 5 a). The results showed that the ζ-potentials of the Ce-CDs/AuNPs and Au@HgNPs, with the addition of AFB1, are −37.3 mV and −4.7 mV, −13.3 mV, respectively. These results clearly indicated that the charge transfer complex leads to the generation of Au@HgNPs, forming a “hot spot” through MG-induced Au@HgNPs aggregation for the enhancement of the SERS signal; AFB1 inhibited the aggregation of Au@HgNPs reduced the SERS signal.

To confirm the formation of Au@HgNPs, UV–vis spectra were recorded after the synthesis of Ce-CDs/AuNPs. As shown in Fig. 5b, the absorption maximum at 526 nm indicates that Au@HgNPs has been successfully formed. In comparison with the blue shift of the absorption peak at 546 nm with Ce-CDs/AuNPs, the lattice fringe spacing of the gold nanoparticles decreased from 0.26 nm to 0.21 nm, which was verified by HRTEM, indicating that the reduction of nanoparticle size is responsible for the blue shift of the absorption band of AuNPs (Du et al., 2016).

Au-Hg bonds were formed in the system of Ce-CDs/AuNPs + Hg2+, and Au@HgNPs oxidized LMG without SERS signal to MG with SERS signal. Adding Ce-CDs/AuNPs to oxidize LMG first and then adding Hg2+ played a synergistic effect, and the results showed that the formation of Au-Hg amalgam oxidized LMG with a stronger SERS signal as shown in Fig. 5c.

In the reaction system, the nanozyme activity acts mainly because of the generation of reactive radicals such as ∙O2 –. In the catalytic reaction with Hg2+, the generation of active oxygen (ROS) radicals was investigated by validation experiments. The generation of superoxide radicals (∙O2 –) was investigated in the catalytic reaction with iodonitrotetrazolium violet INT. As shown in Fig. 5d, when INT was added to the Ce-CDs/AuNPs + AFB1 + Hg2+ system, INT was generated by electron transfer in the presence of ∙O2 – with UV absorption at 638 nm, suggesting that ∙O2 – was the main reactive radical in this system. After adding AFB1, the absorbance was significantly lower than that of Au@HgNPs system, also confirming the OXD-like activity of AFB1 by inhibiting the aggregation of Au@HgNPs and thus reducing the OXD-like activity.

We used electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) technology to detect ROS in the reaction system. As shown in Fig. 5e, the EPR spectra demonstrates that ∙O2 – were produced in the Ce-CDs/AuNPs + Hg2++system. It is noticed that the peak intensities of ∙O2 – in the Au@HgNPs system are higher than that in the Ce-CDs/AuNPs system, demonstrating the higher concentrations of ROS is generated by Hg2+ to induce an improved OXD-like activity.

According to the sample pretreatment section, the Hg2+ was measured using the constructed SERS sensor after adding standard Hg2+ concentrations to the actual samples (Zhang et al., 2022). The recoveries of Hg2+ spiked (0.83, 8.33, and 15 µg L-1) ranged from 89.15 to 109.63%, as shown in Table S4. The RSD value for the SERS modes was less than 5.54%, as shown in Table S2, which indicates the good accuracy of SERS mode according to GB5009.17-2014. All the above results can show that the developed strategy can be used to go for the detection of Hg2+ in actual samples.

The sample peanut oil was pretreated and the analysis of the actual sample with the constructed sensor was verified by the standard addition recovery method. The results are shown in Table S5. The recoveries of standard addition ranged from 93.97% to 109.30%, indicating that the strategy can be used to quantify AFB1 in peanut oil samples. The RSD value for the SERS modes was less than 5.61%, as shown in Table S3, which indicates the good accuracy of SERS mode according to GB 5009.22-2016.

In conclusion, a novel SERS nanoprobe for the detection of Hg2+ and aflatoxin B1 switching strategies was developed based on the formation of Au-Hg amalgam, which enhances the OXD activity of AuNPs, oxidizes the Raman activity reporter white LMG to SERS-active MG, and leads to the production of Au@HgNPs, forming “hot spots” for SERS signal enhancement. The proposed SERS sensing has high sensitivity with LOD of 0.147 μg L−1 for Hg2+ and 0.08 μg L−1 for AFB1, with good selectivity and reproducibility. The proposed method provides a new general strategy for Raman-activated mercury ions.

Xiaorong Zhao: Writing – original draft, Methodology. Qiulan Li: Validation, Methodology. Hong Li: Methodology, Investigation. Yijie Wang: Validation, Methodology. Feijian Xiao: Investigation. Dezhi Yang: Writing – review & editing, Software, Resources. Qinghai Xia: Resources. Yaling Yang: Writing – review & editing, Software.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was greatly supported by Yunnan Fundamental Research Projects (No. 202201AU070117), Yunnan Fundamental Research Projects (No. 202001AT070002), Major Science and Technology Project of Yunnan Province (No. 202002AE320006), Joint Specialized Fund for Applied Fundamental Research Program of the Department of Science and Technology of Yunnan Province – Kunming Medical University (No. 2018FE001(-133)).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.136443.

ce:section.ce:para:
